'''Glen Tomlinson''' (born 18 March 1970) is an Australian former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played in the 1990s and 2000s. He played at club level for [[Batley Bulldogs]] (two spells), [[Bradford Bulls]], [[Hull FC]] and [[Wakefield Trinity|Wakefield Trinity Wildcats]] as a {{rlp|fe}} or {{rlp|hb}}. A stand at Batley Bulldogs' ground, [[Mount Pleasant, Batley|Mount Pleasant]] is named after Glen Tomlinson.<ref name="Edgar, Harry 2008">Edgar, Harry (2008). ''Rugby League Journal Annual 2009 [Page-47]''. Rugby League Journal Publishing. {{ISBN|978-0-9548355-4-5}}</ref>

==Playing career==
===Batley===
Born in [[Redcliffe, Queensland]],<ref>{{cite news |last1=Tingle |first1=Richard |title=The new 'Bluey' |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0003600/19980121/053/0053 |work=Hull Daily Mail |date=21 January 1998 |page=61 |via=[[British Newspaper Archive]] |url-access=subscription}}</ref> Tomlinson began his [[rugby league]] career with his hometown club, [[Redcliffe Dolphins]]. He moved to England in 1991, signing for [[Batley Bulldogs|Batley]]. He scored 124 tries during his two spells with Batley, breaking the Batley club record for tries in a career, which had been set by [[Wharton Davies|Wattie Davies]] way back in 1912, who had scored 122 tries in 448 matches. He held the record until 2006, when he was surpassed by [[Craig Lingard]].<ref>{{cite web|last1=Hunt|first1=Andrew|title=Role model Lingard delighted with new record|url=http://www.batleynews.co.uk/sport/local-sport/role-model-lingard-delighted-with-new-record-1-1321855gby_league/2311095.stm|website=Batley and Birstall News|publisher=Johnston Press|accessdate=28 July 2014|date=16 May 2006}}{{Dead link|date=December 2019 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }}</ref>

===Bradford Bulls===
In January 1996, Tomlinson was signed by [[Bradford Bulls]] in an exchange deal, with three Bradford players (Phil Hardwick, Dave Turpin and [[Roger Simpson]]) moving to Batley.<ref>{{cite news |title=Tomlinson moves to Bulls |work=The Guardian|date=23 January 1996 |location=London |page=19 |id={{ProQuest|187832377}}}}</ref>

He played in the [[1997 Challenge Cup]] final defeat against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]].

===Wakefield Trinity Wildcats===
In 2000, at the height of a financial crisis at Wakefield Trinity Wildcats, the contracts of all players aged over 24 were terminated during September 2000. The players affected were; [[Andy Fisher (rugby league)|Andy Fisher]], [[Bobbie Goulding]], [[Warren Jowitt]], [[Tony Kemp (rugby league)|Tony Kemp]] ([[player-coach]]), [[Steve McNamara]], [[Francis Maloney (rugby league)|Francis Maloney]], [[Martin Masella]], [[Steve Prescott]], [[Bright Sodje]], [[Francis Stephenson]] and Glen Tomlinson.<ref name="Goodway calls for aid package">{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/1295270.stm|title=Goodway calls for aid package|publisher=BBC|date=24 April 2001|access-date=1 January 2012}}</ref>

He returned to Batley, where he finished his career, and was named [[Northern Ford Premiership]] Player of the Year in 2002.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Irvine |first1=Christopher |title=Aspinwall's inside job crucial for wounded Warriors |url=https://www.thetimes.com/article/aspinwalls-inside-job-crucial-for-wounded-warriors-9q5p2zq2vk8 |website=[[The Times]] |access-date=11 June 2024 |date=9 October 2002}}</ref>
